[concat('Region to deploy solution resources -- separate from location selection',parameters('location'))]
Workspace name for Log Analytics where Microsoft Sentinel is setup

Name of the pre-existing blob container inside provided Storage Account.
Name of the pre-existing Key Vault with Titan API credentials. The credentials are expected under following keys: TitanUserNameGraph nad TitanAPIKeyGraph
Security product to which the indicators will be applied. Allowed values: Azure Sentinel, Microsoft Defender ATP.
The action to apply if the indicator is matched from within the targetProduct security tool. Allowed values: unknown, allow, block, alert.
How many days of history should be pulled on the first run. Leave 0 to start from the current time.

[[variables('AzureBlobConnectionName')]

headers.User-Agent: Intel 471 - Malware Intelligence Graph - Azure Logic App 1.0.0

metadata.title: [Deprecated] Intel 471 Malware Intelligence to Graph Security
metadata.description: This playbook ingests malware indicators from Intel 471's Titan API into Microsoft Graph Security as tiIndicator resource type.
prerequisites: 1. An active account in Titan platform, which is available as part of Intel 471's subscriptions. For more information, please contact sales@intel471.com.

3. Pre-existing [Key Vault](https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/general/basic-concepts) for securely storing Titan API credentials. Store Titan API credentials as secrets under `TitanUserNameGraph` and `TitanAPIKeyGraph` keys.

4. Pre-existing [Storage account](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/storage-account-overview) with blob container already created for persisting data such as cursor between the API calls.
5. Threat Intelligence connector enabled in Sentinel. Go to Sentinel instance → 'Content hub' and install 'Threat Intelligence' solution.
postDeployment: 1. Go to the Key Vault. Select `Access control (IAM)` → `+ Add` → `Add role assignment`. Choose `Key Vault Secrets User`. On the next screen hit `+ Select members`, search for Intel 471 and select newly created logic app. Select it and proceed with granting access rights.

2. Go to created `Logic App` → `Edit`. Navigate to `CollectAndSubmitIndicators → SubmitInBatches → Connections` block. Inside this block create a new connection. Select `Connect with managed identity`, provide connection name and click `Create`.

3. Grant `ThreatIndicators.ReadWrite.OwnedBy` role for the Logic App to allow indicator ingestion through the tiIndicators API. It can't be done in the Azure portal at the moment. You can use the Azure CLI script listed below instead. Consult [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/app-service/scenario-secure-app-access-microsoft-graph-as-app?tabs=azure-cli#grant-access-to-microsoft-graph) for more details.
4. Optionally change the schedule's frequency in `Recurrence` block (the first one).

properties.displayName: Intel471-ImportMalwareIntelligenceToGraphSecurity

metadata.description: Name of the pre-existing Storage Account where Logic App can store helper data.
Name of the pre-existing blob container inside provided Storage Account.

Name of the pre-existing Key Vault with API credentials. For Titan backend, use keys: TitanUserNameSentinel and TitanAPIKeySentinel. For Verity backend, use keys: VerityUserNameSentinel and VerityAPIKeySentinel
ID of the Log Analytics workspace to which the indicators will be directed.
How many days of history should be pulled on the first run. Leave 0 to start from the current time.
Select the Intel 471 backend to use: Titan or Verity

[[variables('AzureBlobConnectionName')]
[[variables('AzureKeyVaultConnectionName')]

headers.User-Agent: Intel 471 - Malware Intelligence Sentinel - Azure Logic App 4.0.0

select.name: @item()['data']['threat']['data']['family']
select.pattern: @if(equals(item()?['data']?['indicator_type'], 'url'), concat('[url:value = ''', item()?['data']?['indicator_data']?['url'], ''']'), if(equals(item()?['data']?['indicator_type'], 'ipv4'), concat('[ipv4-addr:value = ''', item()?['data']?['indicator_data']?['address'], ''']'),    concat('[file:hashes.md5 = ''', item()?['data']?['indicator_data']?['file']?['md5'], ''' OR file:hashes.sha1 = ''', item()?['data']?['indicator_data']?['file']?['sha1'], ''' OR file:hashes.sha256 = ''', item()?['data']?['indicator_data']?['file']?['sha256'], ''']')      ))

metadata.title: Intel 471 Malware Intelligence to Sentinel
metadata.description: This playbook ingests malware indicators from Intel 471's Titan or Verity API into Microsoft Sentinel as tiIndicator resource type.
prerequisites: 1. An active account in Titan or Verity platform, which is available as part of Intel 471's subscriptions. For more information, please contact sales@intel471.com.

3. Pre-existing [Key Vault](https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/key-vault/general/basic-concepts) for securely storing API credentials. Store Titan API credentials as secrets under `TitanUserNameSentinel` and `TitanAPIKeySentinel` keys, or Verity API credentials under `VerityUserNameSentinel` and `VerityAPIKeySentinel` keys.

4. Pre-existing [Storage account](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/storage-account-overview) with blob container already created for persisting data such as cursor between the API calls.
5. Threat Intelligence connector enabled in Sentinel. Go to Sentinel instance → 'Content hub' and install 'Threat Intelligence' solution.
postDeployment: 1. Go to the Key Vault. Select `Access control (IAM)` → `+ Add` → `Add role assignment`. Choose `Key Vault Secrets User`. On the next screen hit `+ Select members`, search for Intel 471 and select newly created logic app. Select it and proceed with granting access rights.
2. Go to the selected Log Analytics workspace and repeat step 1. except grant role `Microsoft Sentinel Contributor`.
3. Optionally change the schedule's frequency in `Recurrence` block (the first one).

properties.displayName: Intel471-ImportMalwareIntelligenceToSentinel
AnyDesk Execution from Abnormal Folder - Potential Malicious Use of RMM Tool
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where Activity has "Process"
| where NewProcessName endswith "AnyDesk.exe"
| where not(NewProcessName has_any (
  "\\AppData\\",
  "\\Downloads\\",
  "Program Files (x86)\\anydesk",
  "Program Files\\anydesk"
  )
)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, processID=tolong(NewProcessId), parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId), EventData 
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: Anydesk is a common and widely utilized tool for remotely controlling machines. However, it has also been adopted by many actors to remotely access victim machines and deploying malware or ransomware payloads. This Hunt Package is designed to exclude common paths where AnyDesk is executed from. Due to AnyDesk being able to be installed or executed from nearly any directory, Analysts should review whether AnyDesk is allowed, and follow internal policies if it is not authorized. Some common "red flag" type directories can be temporary directories, ProgramData and System32. Additionally, to appear more legitimate, some attackers may utilize installation paths that include legitimate sounding names, such as "Microsoft Management" or "Customer Service".

properties.displayName: AnyDesk Execution from Abnormal Folder - Potential Malicious Use of RMM Tool
AnyDesk Service Installation - Potentially Malicious RMM Tool Installation
properties.query:
Event
| where EventID == "7045"
| extend EventData = parse_xml(EventData).DataItem.EventData.Data
| mv-expand bagexpansion=array EventData
| extend EventName=tostring(EventData['@Name']), EventValue=EventData['#text']
| evaluate pivot(EventName, any(EventValue), TimeGenerated, Computer, EventID)
| where ImagePath has "AnyDesk" or ServiceName has_any (  "AnyDesk", "AnyDeskMSI" )

tags.name: description
tags.value: Identifies when the AnyDesk service is installed onto a system. This can be legitimate if the organization allows AnyDesk, however if it is not a commonly utilized application, any service installations should be considered suspect.

properties.displayName: AnyDesk Service Installation - Potentially Malicious RMM Tool Installation
Autorun or ASEP Registry Key Modification
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where ObjectName has_any (
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\\Run",
  "\\software\\Wow6432Node\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\\Run",
  "\\software\\WOW6432Node\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run"
) or ObjectName has_any (
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Explorer\\User Shell Folders",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Explorer\\Shell Folders",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\Userinit",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\Shell",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\\AppInit_DLLs",
  "\\software\\Wow6432Node\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\\AppInit_DLLs",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\\Load",
  "\\software\\Wow6432Node\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\\Load",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Policies\\Explorer\\Run",
  "\\Image File Execution Options\\vds.exe\\debugger"
)
| where not ObjectValueName has_any (
  "\\Microsoft Visual Studio\\Installer\\",
  "\\ClickToRun\\OfficeClickToRun.exe",
  "\\software\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Winlogon\\Shell",
  "\\Microsoft\\OneDrive\\",
  "\\Windows\\system32\\SearchIndexer.exe",
  "\\Microsoft\\Teams\\",
  "\\Microsoft\\Edge\\Application\\"
)
| where not ParentProcessName has_any (
  "Program Files (x86)\\Microsoft\\Edge\\Application",
  "Program Files\\Microsoft OneDrive\\",
  "Program Files\\WindowsApps\\MSTeams"
)
| where not NewValue has_any (
  "\\Windows\\System32\\ctfmon.exe"
)
| where not NewValue contains "\\ProgramData\\Package Cache\\" and ParentProcessName has_any (
  "vc_redist.x86.exe",
  "vc_redist.x64.exe"
)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, processID=tolong(NewProcessId), parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId), EventData 
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: A common method that adversaries and malicious software alike achieve persistence is by adding a program to the startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key. Adding an entry to the "run keys" in the Registry or startup folder allows the referenced program to be executed when a user logs in. They are often utilized for legitimate purposes, however when utilized maliciously the key name/value is often obviously suspicious, such as random names, or objects loaded from temp or public folders.

properties.displayName: Autorun or ASEP Registry Key Modification
AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) Discovery
properties.query:
AWSCloudTrail
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where EventName has_any (
  "ListRoles",
  "ListIdentities",
  "ListAccountAliases",
  "GetUser",
  "GetCallerIdentity"
)
| project TimeGenerated, EventSource, EventName, UserIdentityType, UserIdentityArn, UserIdentityAccountId, UserIdentityUserName, UserAgent, IpAddress, timeWindowSeen = bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| summarize FirstSeen=min(TimeGenerated), LastSeen=max(TimeGenerated), EventSources=make_set(EventSource), ApiCalls=make_set(EventName), UserIdentityTypes=make_set(UserIdentityType), UserName=make_set(UserIdentityUserName), AccountId=make_set(UserIdentityAccountId), UserAgents=make_set(UserAgent), SrcIPs=make_set(IpAddress), distinctApiCnt=dcount(EventName) by UserIdentityArn, timeWindowSeen
| where distinctApiCnt > 1
| order by timeWindowSeen

tags.name: description
tags.value: This Hunt Package identifies activity that could be associated with discovery behaviors targeting AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM).

properties.displayName: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) Discovery
Browser Spawning Suspicious Applications - Potential Exploit or Social Engineering
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where ParentProcessName has_any (
  "\\chrome.exe",
  "\\iexplore.exe",
  "\\edge.exe",
  "\\opera.exe",
  "\\firefox.exe",
  "\\brave.exe",
  "\\msedgewebview2.exe",
  "\\msedge.exe"
)
| where NewProcessName has_any (
  "\\cmd.exe",
  "\\powershell.exe",
  "\\bash.exe",
  "\\sh.exe",
  "\\python.exe",
  "\\mshta.exe",
  "\\curl.exe",
  "\\wget.exe"
)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, processID=tolong(NewProcessId), parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId), EventData 
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: Exploitation of browsers often involves performing commands as a result of the exploitation. This Hunt Package focuses on commonly utilized scripting engines and other applications that can be abused from browser exploitation to retrieve, install or perform malicious commands. Additionally, browsers were found to be susceptible to a File Explorer attack, where a user is prompted to open a File Explorer window (commonly associated with uploading a file) and copy/paste a string into the File Explorer window. This action executes the command provided by the attacker. This technique was dubbed FileFix, which follows similar social engineering techniques to ClickFix.

properties.displayName: Browser Spawning Suspicious Applications - Potential Exploit or Social Engineering

properties.description: creating-a-shadow-copy_HuntingQueries Hunting Query with template version 4.0.0

properties.displayName: Creating a Shadow Copy
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, tostring(EventID), CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId), SubjectUserName, SubjectLogonId, SubjectUserSid, SourceComputerId
| where (CommandLine has "create" and CommandLine contains "shadow") or CommandLine has "Checkpoint-Computer"
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: This Threat Hunt package identifies suspicious activities related shadow copies being created from a variety of techniques.

properties.displayName: Creating a Shadow Copy
Dump LSASS via Renamed procdump
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where NewProcessName endswith ".exe"
| where CommandLine has "lsass"
| where CommandLine has_any ("-ma","-mm")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, processID=tolong(NewProcessId), parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId), EventData 
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: Identify ProcDump usage as a means to dump LSASS data. ProcDump is a SysInternals tool that can be used to dump process memory, dumping the process memory of lsass.exe which can be used to obtain credentials.

properties.displayName: Dump LSASS via Renamed procdump
Execution BAT Script to Unpack Payload
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, tostring(EventID), CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId), SubjectUserName, SubjectLogonId, SubjectUserSid, SourceComputerId
| order by TimeGenerated
| where CommandLine contains "cmd.exe"
| where CommandLine contains "/c"
| where CommandLine contains ".bat"
| where CommandLine has_any (
  "AppData",
  "Downloads",
  "Temp"
)

tags.name: description
tags.value: This Hunt Package is meant to identify a nuanced method of execution of a .bat file that can be indicative of an unpacking sequence that leads to the deployment of an additional executable.

properties.displayName: Execution BAT Script to Unpack Payload
File Created In Startup Folder
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(1d)
| where EventID == "4663"
| where SubjectUserName !endswith "$"
| where ObjectName has @"Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup\"
| where not(Process endswith "SearchProtocolHost.exe")
| where EventData has_any ("0x2", "%%4417")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, EventID, ObjectName, Process, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), SubjectUserName

tags.name: description
tags.value: This package is designed to detect the activity around a file being created and put in the Windows Startup Folder.

properties.displayName: File Created In Startup Folder
Java Spawning Child Process by Unique Child Process Name - Potential Exploitation Activity
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where ParentProcessName contains "java"
| where isnotempty(Process)
| summarize firstEvent=min(TimeGenerated), lastEvent=max(TimeGenerated), sourceHosts=make_set(Computer), sourceComputerIds=make_set(SourceComputerId), eventIds=make_set(tostring(EventID)), processCommandLines=make_set(CommandLine), parentProcessPaths=make_set(ParentProcessName), processIds=make_set(NewProcessId), parentprocessIds=make_set(ProcessId), eventDatas=make_set(EventData), count() by ParentProcessName, NewProcessName| where count_ < 50
| order by count_ asc
| project count_, firstEvent, lastEvent, sourceHosts, ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, eventIds, processCommandLines, processIds, parentprocessIds, eventDatas

tags.name: description
tags.value: Identifies when Java spawns a child process and presents the data by most unique parent/child process first for the timeframe selected in the hunt query. The uniqueness of the child process can be an indication of unexpected activity being performed by Java, potentially by an exploit. The provided query is designed to return all java process executions, and therefore will return legitimate results. This is intended to catch the unknown child processes that may be unique to the attacker or target. The query will sort by uniqueness first and if supported by the tool, will limit unique counts returned, to help identify outliers. This package was originally inspired by the Log4Shell, Spring4Shell, and Text4Shell vulnerabilities released in late 2021 and 2022, respectively.

properties.displayName: Java Spawning Child Process by Unique Child Process Name - Potential Exploitation Activity
MeshAgent Suspicious Child Process - Potential Malicious RMM Tool Usage
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where ParentProcessName has "meshagent.exe" and NewProcessName has_any (
  "cmd.exe",
  "powershell.exe",
  "pwsh.exe",
  "bcdedit.exe",
  "schtasks.exe"
) or NewProcessName has "meshagent.exe" and CommandLine has_any (
  "-kvm1",
  "-b64exec"
)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, processID=tolong(NewProcessId), parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId), EventData 
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: Identifies the default installation process executing suspect applications, such as powershell, schtasks, cmd and other applications that can by abused by built in modules.

properties.displayName: MeshAgent Suspicious Child Process - Potential Malicious RMM Tool Usage
Methods for Downloading Files with PowerShell
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, tostring(EventID), CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId), SubjectUserName, SubjectLogonId, SubjectUserSid, SourceComputerId
| where NewProcessName has_any (
  "powershell.exe",
  "pwsh.exe",
  "powershell_ise.exe",
  "pwsh-preview.exe"
)
| where CommandLine has_any (
  "Invoke-WebRequest",
  " iwr",
  "curl",
  "wget",
  "Invoke-RestMethod",
  " irm",
  "Start-BitsTransfer",
  " sbt",
  "System.Net.WebClient",
  "System.Net.Http.HttpClient"
)
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: This threat hunt package identifies instances where PowerShell is being used to download files from external sources, a common technique used in malware delivery and lateral movement. The hunt examines various methods by which PowerShell can be leveraged for file downloads, including the use of cmdlets such as Invoke-WebRequest (iwr), Invoke-RestMethod (irm), and Start-BitsTransfer (sbt), as well as direct utilization of .NET classes like System.Net.WebClient and HttpClient. The package also checks for potentially suspicious use of aliases (curl, wget) and other common executables that invoke PowerShell scripts to download malicious payloads.

properties.displayName: Methods for Downloading Files with PowerShell
NetSupport Manager Execution from Abnormal Folder - Potential Malicious Use of RMM Tool
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where NewProcessName endswith "client32.exe"
| where not(NewProcessName has_any (
  "Program Files (x86)\\NetSupport",
  "Program Files\\NetSupport",
  "\\ProgramData\\NetSupport\\",
  "\\AppData\\Local\\NetSupport\\"
  )
)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, processID=tolong(NewProcessId), parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId), EventData 
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: NetSupport is a common and widely utilized tool for remotely controlling machines, and was even utilized as the base code for the NetSupport RAT. It has been adopted by many actors to remotely access victim machines and deploying additional malware or ransomware payloads. This Hunt Package is designed to exclude common paths where NetSupport is executed from. Due to NetSupport being able to be installed or executed from nearly any directory, Analysts should review whether NetSupport is allowed, and follow internal policies if it is not authorized. Some common "red flag" type directories can be temporary directories, Temp and System32. Additionally, to appear more legitimate, some attackers may utilize installation paths that include legitimate sounding names, such as "Microsoft Management" or "Customer Service".

properties.displayName: NetSupport Manager Execution from Abnormal Folder - Potential Malicious Use of RMM Tool
Potential Maldoc Execution Chain Observed
properties.query:
let officeProducts = dynamic(["WINWORD.EXE","EXCEL.EXE","POWERPNT.EXE","MSACCESS.EXE","VISIO.EXE","WINPROJ.EXE"]);
let executionMethods = dynamic(["powershell.exe","cmd.exe","WScript.exe","rundll32.exe","cscript.exe","wmic.exe","mshta.exe","msiexec.exe"]);
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where (NewProcessName has_any (officeProducts,"OUTLOOK.EXE","explorer.exe",executionMethods) or ParentProcessName has_any (officeProducts,"OUTLOOK","explorer",executionMethods))
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, EventID, CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId)
| extend sourceAppTrue=case(NewProcessName endswith "outlook.exe",1,
                            (ParentProcessName has_any("outlook.exe","explorer.exe") and NewProcessName has_any(officeProducts)),1,
                            0)
| extend officeAppTrue=case(NewProcessName has_any(officeProducts),1,
                            ParentProcessName has_any(officeProducts),1,
                            0)
| extend executionTrue=case(NewProcessName has_any(executionMethods),1,
                            ParentProcessName has_any(executionMethods),1,
                            0)
| summarize process=make_set(NewProcessName,10), processId=make_set(processId,10), parentProcess=make_set(ParentProcessName,10),parentProcessId=make_set(parentProcessId,10), sourceApp=sum(sourceAppTrue), officeApp=sum(officeAppTrue), execution=sum(executionTrue)
 by Computer, bin(TimeGenerated,5m)
| where sourceApp > 0 and officeApp > 0 and execution > 0

tags.name: description
tags.value: Detect the aftermath of a successfully delivered and executed maldoc (Microsoft Office). A detection indicates an Office document was opened from an email or download/link, spawned a suspicious execution, and attempted to execute code via common Windows binaries (i.e. powershell, cmd, rundll32, etc).

properties.displayName: Potential Maldoc Execution Chain Observed
PowerShell Encoded Command Execution
properties.query:
union 
(
    SecurityEvent
    | project 
        TimeGenerated,
        DeviceName=Computer,
        EventID=tostring(EventID),
        ParentProcessName,
        ProcessName=NewProcessName,
        CommandLine,
        SubjectUserName,
        SourceComputerId,
        processID=tolong(NewProcessId),
        parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId)
),
(
    DeviceEvents
    | project
        TimeGenerated,
        DeviceName,
        ProcessName=InitiatingProcessFolderPath,
        CommandLine=ProcessCommandLine,
        SubjectUserName=InitiatingProcessAccountName,
        SourceComputerId=DeviceId,
        processID=InitiatingProcessId,
        parentProcessID=InitiatingProcessId,
        ParentProcessName=InitiatingProcessParentFileName,
        AdditionalFields,
        EventID=ActionType
)
| where 
    ProcessName has_any (
      "powershell.exe",
      "powershell_ise.exe", 
      "pwsh.exe"
      )
| extend CommandLine_ = tostring(AdditionalFields.Command)
| extend AllCommandLine=coalesce(CommandLine_,CommandLine)
| where AllCommandLine matches regex "-([Ee\\^]{1,2}[NnCcOoDdEeMmAaPpHh\\^` ]+|[Ee])\\s+\"?[a-zA-Z0-9+/=]{10,}"
| where not (ParentProcessName has "Plugins\\Microsoft.GuestConfiguration.ConfigurationforWindows" and ParentProcessName endswith "gc_worker.exe")
| order by TimeGenerated
| project TimeGenerated,DeviceName,EventID,ParentProcessName,ProcessName,AllCommandLine,SubjectUserName,processID,parentProcessID,SourceComputerId

tags.name: description
tags.value:
Looks for valid variations of the -EncodedCommand parameter. This is commonly used to encode or obfuscate commands, and not all occurrences are malicious. For example, benign complex commands may require encoding to properly run on a target system. Analysis of the encoded command by base64 decoding the encoded data will be necessary. 

NOTE: When excessive results are received, notes provided in the Deployment Steps may be able to aid in tuning the dataset, per tool. Some general suggestions may be to group by parent processes to identify common, approved and legitimate, applications; group by common line and only review command lines observed less than 3 times, across 3 hosts (indicating single execution across multiple hosts); or limit the results by distinct number of hosts and event counts, such as limiting to less than 5 observed hosts or total execution count.

properties.displayName: PowerShell Encoded Command Execution
Powershell History Modification or Deletion
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, EventID, CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId)
| where CommandLine has_any("Clear-History","del (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath*","rm (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath","Remove-Item (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath","Set-PSReadlineOption -HistorySaveStyle SaveNothing")

tags.name: description
tags.value: Adversaries may attempt to clear the command history of compromised hosts to conceal the actions that they have performed during an attack. This content will detect if PowerShell's history was cleared, disabled, or modified.

properties.displayName: Powershell History Modification or Deletion
Python Executing from Non-Standard Directory
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, tostring(EventID), CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId), SubjectUserName, SubjectLogonId, SubjectUserSid, SourceComputerId
| where NewProcessName matches regex ".*python(?:[23](?:\\.\\d+)?w?)?" 
| where not(NewProcessName matches regex @".*(C:\\Python|\\Program Files\\Python|\\WindowsApps\\PythonSoftwareFoundation|\\AppData\\Local\\Programs\\Python|\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\WindowsApps|/usr/bin/|/usr/local/bin/|/opt/python/|/\\.local/bin/|/\\.pyenv/versions/|/miniconda3/bin/|/anaconda3/bin/|/snap/bin/|/opt/homebrew/bin/|/usr/local/opt/python|/Library.*/Frameworks/Python.*\\.framework).*")
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: This Threat Hunt package identifies suspicious Python executions originating from non-standard directories, such as hidden or unconventional locations signaling potential malware infection.

properties.displayName: Python Executing from Non-Standard Directory
Remote Atera Agent Download - Command Line
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, tostring(EventID), CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId), SubjectUserName, SubjectLogonId, SubjectUserSid, SourceComputerId
| where CommandLine has "atera.com"
| where CommandLine has "GetAgent"
| where CommandLine has "integratorLogin"
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: This Threat Hunt package identifies when a tool like a lolbin is used to fetch the Atera's Remote Montoring and Management (RMM) agent directly from Atera's distribution domain.

properties.displayName: Remote Atera Agent Download - Command Line
Scheduled Task Executing from Abnormal Location
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == "4698"
| extend EventData = parse_xml(EventData).EventData.Data
| mv-expand bagexpansion=array EventData
| extend EventName=tostring(EventData['@Name']), EventValue=EventData['#text']
// Add additional original event fields here to carry over to the final output. Ensure to add here and in the project statement
| evaluate pivot(EventName, any(EventValue), TimeGenerated, Computer, EventID, SourceComputerId, EventOriginId)
| extend TaskContent=replace_string(tostring(TaskContent), '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-16"?>', ' ')
| extend TaskContent = parse_xml(TaskContent).Task
// Add filters as necessary using field names from the TaskContent field in the form of TaskContent.Actions.Exec.Command as an example
| where TaskContent.Actions.Exec has_any (
  "Users", 
  "Public", 
  "Perflogs",
  "$Recycle",
  "ProgramData",
  "\\Fonts\\"
  )
| where not(TaskContent.Actions.Exec has_any("\\Windows Defender\\", "\\Microsoft\\Windows\\Application", "\\Microsoft\\ClickToRun"))
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, EventID, SubjectUserName, TaskName, TaskContent.Actions.Exec.Command, TaskContent.Actions.Exec.Arguments, TaskContent, SourceComputerId, EventOriginId
| sort by TimeGenerated desc

tags.name: description
tags.value: This hunt package is designed to capture activity associated with a scheduled task which includes abnormal locations in its details for execution. This is often a mark of persistence or malicious tasks created by malware or attackers. details.

properties.displayName: Scheduled Task Executing from Abnormal Location
Shadow Copies Deletion Using Operating Systems Utilities
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where CommandLine contains "shadow" and CommandLine contains "delete"
| where (NewProcessName has_any (
  "powershell.exe",
  "wmic.exe",
  "vssadmin.exe",
  "vssvc.exe"
) or CommandLine has_any (
  "wmic",
  "vssadmin",
  "vssvc"
))
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, processID=tolong(NewProcessId), parentProcessID=tolong(ProcessId), EventData| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: Ransomware is known to delete Windows shadow copies before it begins encrypting the data on the victim host. This tactic is typically carried out with powershell, vssadmin or wmic. This package identifies activity by powershell, wmic, vssadmin or vssvc with command line arguments containing delete and variations of shadow.

properties.displayName: Shadow Copies Deletion Using Operating Systems Utilities
Suspect Child Process to IIS Worker Process (w3wp.exe) - Potential Exploitation
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, EventID, CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId)
| where ParentProcessName endswith "w3wp.exe"
| where NewProcessName has_any ("cmd.exe", "powershell.exe", "bitsadmin.exe", "net.exe", "wmic.exe", "rundll32.exe", "mshta.exe", "schtasks.exe", "wscript.exe")

tags.name: description
tags.value: This package is intended to identify abnormal child processes to the Microsoft IIS Worker Process (w3wp.exe). The w3wp process runs Web applications, and is responsible for handling requests sent to a Web Server for a specific application pool. Child processes such as cmd.exe or powershell.exe can be an indication of unexpected activity by the process as the result of an exploit attempt.

properties.displayName: Suspect Child Process to IIS Worker Process (w3wp.exe) - Potential Exploitation
Suspicious Child Process for Java - Potential Exploitation Activity
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where ParentProcessName endswith "java.exe"
| where Process has_any (
  "schtasks.exe",
  "cscript.exe",
  "cmd.exe",
  "powershell.exe",
  "wscript.exe",
  "regsvr32.exe",
  "hh.exe",
  "wmic.exe",
  "mshta.exe",
  "rundll32.exe",
  "msiexec.exe",
  "forfiles.exe",
  "scriptrunner.exe",
  "svchost.exe",
  "control.exe",
  "whoami.exe"
  ) 
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, tostring(EventID), ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, Process, CommandLine, SubjectUserName, SourceComputerId, NewProcessId, ProcessId, EventData| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: Identifies when Java spawns unusual child processes, which can be an indication of exploitation of the Java process. Although Java may not be the target process of an exploit, it can be coaxed into executing malicious code as the result of an exploit in another service, such as Log4j or Spring-Core.

properties.displayName: Suspicious Child Process for Java - Potential Exploitation Activity
User Added to Default Privileged Windows Security Groups
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, tostring(EventID), CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId), SubjectUserName, SubjectLogonId, SubjectUserSid, SourceComputerId
| where CommandLine has_all("net","/add")
| where CommandLine has_any (
  "group",
  "localgroup"
)
| where CommandLine has_any ( 
  "Administrators",
  "Domain Admins",
  "DnsAdmins",
  "Enterprise Admins",
  "Hyper-V Administrators",
  "Schema Admins"
)
| order by TimeGenerated

tags.name: description
tags.value: This package is designed to capture the activity surrounding the execution of command line arguments that add a user to default privileged Windows Security Groups (local and domain).

properties.displayName: User Added to Default Privileged Windows Security Groups

properties.description: wevtutil-cleared-log_HuntingQueries Hunting Query with template version 4.0.0

properties.displayName: Wevtutil Cleared Log
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, EventID, CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId)
| where NewProcessName endswith "wevtutil.exe"
| where CommandLine has_any("cl","sl","set-log","clear-log")

tags.name: description
tags.value: This use case is designed to detect when "wevtutil" is used to clear logs to potentially obfuscate malicious events occurring prior to the clean up.

properties.displayName: Wevtutil Cleared Log
WMIC Windows Internal Discovery and Enumeration
properties.query:
SecurityEvent
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Activity, EventID, CommandLine, NewProcessName, processId = tolong(NewProcessId), ParentProcessName, parentProcessId = tolong(ProcessId)
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where CommandLine has_all("wmic","path") or 
        CommandLine has_all("wmic","get") or
        CommandLine has_all("wmic","list")
| summarize  CommandLineCnt=dcount(CommandLine), ProcessCommandLine=make_set(CommandLine), FileName=make_set(NewProcessName), ProcessId=make_set(processId), InitiatingProcessFileName=make_set(ParentProcessName), InitiatingProcessId=make_set(parentProcessId)
 by Computer, bin(TimeGenerated,2m)
| where CommandLineCnt>2

tags.name: description
tags.value: This will identify the potentially malicious use of WMI (Windows Management Interface) utilized for local enumeration and discovery of a host.

properties.displayName: WMIC Windows Internal Discovery and Enumeration
Intel471
properties.descriptionHtml:
Note: Please refer to the following before installing the solution:

• Review the solution Release Notes

• There may be known issues pertaining to this Solution, please refer to them before installing.

The Intel 471 solution for Microsoft Sentinel ingests malware indicators into the Log Analytics workspace and provides a curated set of community hunting queries to proactively hunt for threats in Microsoft Sentinel.

Hunting Queries: 25, Playbooks: 2

Learn more about Microsoft Sentinel | Learn more about Solutions
